patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/3] perf record: fix multi-record Zstd compression

version_history.subject: [PATCH 0/3] perf record: fix multi-record Zstd compression

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series fixes a regression in perf record's Zstandard compression feature, which caused it to abort recording and produce an undecompressable file due to a 'Bad address' error. The patches also fix two latent bugs: an output buffer overrun and a broken error fallback in the zstd compressor. The changes ensure that multiple compressed records are handled correctly and that raw data is not stored when compression fails.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 3/3] perf record: Don't store raw data on zstd compression failure

version_history.subject: [PATCH 3/3] perf record: Don't store raw data on zstd compression failure

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the perf record tool's ZSTD compression handling. When ZSTD compression fails, it previously attempted to copy the uncompressed data into the output buffer, which can lead to issues such as incorrect header sizes and unbounded copying. Instead, this patch propagates the error by returning -1 when compression fails, preventing these problems.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 2/3] perf record: Avoid overrunning the zstd output buffer

version_history.subject: [PATCH 2/3] perf record: Avoid overrunning the zstd output buffer

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the zstd compression API used by perf tools, where the output buffer could be overrun when compressing data. The issue occurred because the function did not check if there was enough space left in the output buffer to hold a full record before writing one. The patch adds a check to prevent this overwriting and returns an error instead.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 1/3] perf record: Fix multiple PERF_RECORD_COMPRESSED2 records per push

version_history.subject: [PATCH 1/3] perf record: Fix multiple PERF_RECORD_COMPRESSED2 records per push

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in perf record where multiple PERF_RECORD_COMPRESSED2 records are emitted per push when Zstd compression is enabled and the compressed output exceeds the maximum record size. The fix involves changing how the header size is calculated to prevent underflow and truncation, allowing for correct decompression of the data. The patch also updates the test case to reflect this change.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [Lsf-pc] [LSF/MM/BPF TOPIC][RFC PATCH v4 00/27] Private Memory Nodes (w/ Compressed RAM)

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils] nfsstat: display NFSv4 callback operation statistics

version_history.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils] nfsstat: display NFSv4 callback operation statistics

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new feature to the nfsstat utility, which displays NFSv4 callback operation statistics. The kernel maintains per-operation call counts for NFSv4 backchannel operations and exports them through the server-stats netlink dump. The patch updates the nfsstat code to report these counts in a 'Server nfs v4 callback operations' section when the stats are fetched via netlink. This feature is only available over netlink, as the /proc interface does not expose equivalent data.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 0/6] nfsd/sunrpc: convert nfsstat server-side interfaces to use netlink

version_history.subject: [PATCH v5 0/6] nfsd/sunrpc: convert nfsstat server-side interfaces to use netlink

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 1/6] sunrpc: add per-netns per-procedure call counts to svc_stat
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds per-network namespace (netns) call counts for each RPC procedure to the svc_stat structure in the Linux kernel's sunrpc module. The existing global call count arrays are not netns-aware, which is a problem for the upcoming netlink stats interface that requires namespace-scoped statistics. To solve this issue, the patch introduces per-netns equivalents of the global call count arrays and updates relevant code to manage these new counters. This change will enable more fine-grained monitoring and statistics collection in network namespaces.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 2/6] sunrpc: use per-net counts in svc_seq_show()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the svc_seq_show() function to read procedure call counts from per-network-namespace stat arrays instead of global arrays. This allows /proc/net/rpc/nfsd to report procedure call counts for each network namespace. The change only affects nfsd, which always allocates these arrays, making it safe to use them.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 3/6] nfsd: implement server-stats-get netlink handler
series_items.patch_summary: This patch implements a netlink handler to expose NFS server statistics through the nfsd generic netlink family. The handler uses a dump operation to stream statistics across multiple netlink messages, allowing for efficient retrieval of server statistics. It also includes support for per-version procedure counts and NFSv4 per-operation counts. This allows the nfsstat tool to retrieve server statistics via netlink with a procfs fallback for older kernels.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 4/6] sunrpc: remove unused svc_version vs_count field
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes the unused vs_count field from struct svc_version and its associated percpu counters in various NFS-related modules, as it is no longer accessed by any code. The removal of these fields simplifies the code and reduces memory usage.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 5/6] nfsd: count NFSv4 callback operations per netns
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds per-netns counters to track NFSv4 callback operations in the Linux kernel's NFS server implementation. It introduces a new array of percpu counters for callback operations, indexed by RFC 8881 callback opcode, and updates the nfsd4_run_cb() function to bump the relevant counter for each queued callback. The patch also removes redundant statistics tracking for wdeleg_getattr conflicts and drops related code. This change aims to provide more accurate statistics for NFSv4 backchannel operations.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Chuck Lever noted that the patch introduces a use-after-free (UAF) possibility, specifically in nfsd4_run_cb(), which increments the per-netns callback counter after freeing the callback structure, and requested addressing this issue before applying the patch.
review_comments.raw_body:
I agree with the Sashiko review that this patch introduces a UAF
possibility that needs to be addressed before the patch is applied.


-- 
Chuck Lever

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 6/6] nfsd: export NFSv4 callback op stats via netlink
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for exporting NFSv4 callback operation statistics via netlink, allowing nfsstat to report backchannel operation counts over netlink. The patch introduces a new attribute 'proc4cb-ops' in the server-stats netlink dump, which reuses the existing layout and emits one entry per callback opcode from the per-netns callback counters.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Identified an issue with the loop emitting entries for every opcode, but only those with a nfsd4_callback_ops instance are incremented. Suggested checking what the forechannel counter does for SEQUENCE operations.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, Jun 18, 2026, at 12:58 PM, Jeff Layton wrote:
> Add a proc4cb-ops nested attribute to the server-stats netlink dump,
> reusing the existing server-proc-entry (op/count) layout. The first dump
> message emits one entry per callback opcode (OP_CB_GETATTR..OP_CB_OFFLOAD)
> from the per-netns callback counters; the set is small and fits alongside
> the scalar stats, so no extra paging is needed.
>
> This lets nfsstat report NFSv4 backchannel operation counts over netlink,
> including CB_GETATTR which corresponds to the procfs wdeleg_getattr line.
>
> Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-8
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> ---
>  Documentation/netlink/specs/nfsd.yaml |  6 ++++++
>  fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c                      | 23 +++++++++++++++++++++++
>  include/uapi/linux/nfsd_netlink.h     |  1 +
>  3 files changed, 30 insertions(+)

> diff --git a/fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c b/fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c
> index 9bedbe7096c3..674fab6a86a5 100644
> --- a/fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c
> +++ b/fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c
> @@ -2476,6 +2476,29 @@ int nfsd_nl_server_stats_get_dumpit(struct sk_buff *skb,
>  			}
>  		}
> 
> +#ifdef CONFIG_NFSD_V4
> +		/* NFSv4 callback (backchannel) per-operation counts */
> +		for (int op = OP_CB_GETATTR; op <= OP_CB_OFFLOAD; op++) {
> +			struct nlattr *nest;
> +			u64 cnt;
> +
> +			cnt = percpu_counter_sum_positive(
> +				&nn->cb_counter[op]);

The loop emits an entry for every opcode in OP_CB_GETATTR..OP_CB_OFFLOAD,
but only opcodes with a nfsd4_callback_ops instance get incremented (via
nfsd4_run_cb() keyed on cb->cb_ops->opcode). Opcodes nfsd never sends
correctly report 0. The exception is op 11 / CB_SEQUENCE: it's written by
encode_cb_sequence4args() on every minorversion>0 callback, so it goes
out on the wire but is never counted. The dump always shows op 11 with
count 0, which could be misread as "none sent" rather than "not tracked."

Check what the forechannel counter does for SEQUENCE operations.


-- 
Chuck Lever

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series converts the nfsstat server-side interfaces to use netlink, addressing limitations in the current procfs interface. It adds per-network-namespace procedure call counts to the sunrpc layer and exposes them through a new netlink handler in the nfsd generic netlink family. The patch also includes tracking of callback operation counts.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Raised a potential use-after-free (UAF) possibility in the patch and suggested addressing it before application.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, Jun 18, 2026, at 12:57 PM, Jeff Layton wrote:
> The NFS server tracks per-operation call counts for the forward channel
> (proc4ops) but keeps no statistics for the NFSv4 backchannel (callback)
> operations it sends to clients.
>
> Add a per-netns array of percpu counters for callback operations, indexed
> by RFC 8881 callback opcode (OP_CB_GETATTR..OP_CB_OFFLOAD), and bump the
> relevant counter in nfsd4_run_cb(), which is hit exactly once per callback
> that is actually queued.
>
> CB_GETATTR is sent when a GETATTR conflicts with an outstanding write
> delegation, which is roughly what the dedicated wdeleg_getattr counter
> tracked. The two are not identical: the old counter incremented on every
> such conflict, whereas the CB_GETATTR counter only counts callbacks that
> are actually queued, so concurrent conflicts that coalesce onto an
> already in-flight CB_GETATTR are now counted once rather than once per
> conflict. Report the procfs "wdeleg_getattr" line from the CB_GETATTR
> counter and drop the now-redundant NFSD_STATS_WDELEG_GETATTR counter, its
> helper, and its increment site.
>
> Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-8
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> ---
>  fs/nfsd/netns.h        | 12 +++++++++++-
>  fs/nfsd/nfs4callback.c |  7 ++++++-
>  fs/nfsd/nfs4state.c    |  2 --
>  fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c       | 14 ++++++++++++++
>  fs/nfsd/stats.c        |  2 +-
>  fs/nfsd/stats.h        |  5 +++--
>  6 files changed, 35 insertions(+), 7 deletions(-)

I agree with the Sashiko review that this patch introduces a UAF
possibility that needs to be addressed before the patch is applied.


-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Pointed out an issue with the loop emitting entries for every opcode, but only incrementing those with a nfsd4_callback_ops instance. Suggested checking the forechannel counter's behavior for SEQUENCE operations.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, Jun 18, 2026, at 12:58 PM, Jeff Layton wrote:
> Add a proc4cb-ops nested attribute to the server-stats netlink dump,
> reusing the existing server-proc-entry (op/count) layout. The first dump
> message emits one entry per callback opcode (OP_CB_GETATTR..OP_CB_OFFLOAD)
> from the per-netns callback counters; the set is small and fits alongside
> the scalar stats, so no extra paging is needed.
>
> This lets nfsstat report NFSv4 backchannel operation counts over netlink,
> including CB_GETATTR which corresponds to the procfs wdeleg_getattr line.
>
> Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-8
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> ---
>  Documentation/netlink/specs/nfsd.yaml |  6 ++++++
>  fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c                      | 23 +++++++++++++++++++++++
>  include/uapi/linux/nfsd_netlink.h     |  1 +
>  3 files changed, 30 insertions(+)

> diff --git a/fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c b/fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c
> index 9bedbe7096c3..674fab6a86a5 100644
> --- a/fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c
> +++ b/fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c
> @@ -2476,6 +2476,29 @@ int nfsd_nl_server_stats_get_dumpit(struct sk_buff *skb,
>  			}
>  		}
> 
> +#ifdef CONFIG_NFSD_V4
> +		/* NFSv4 callback (backchannel) per-operation counts */
> +		for (int op = OP_CB_GETATTR; op <= OP_CB_OFFLOAD; op++) {
> +			struct nlattr *nest;
> +			u64 cnt;
> +
> +			cnt = percpu_counter_sum_positive(
> +				&nn->cb_counter[op]);

The loop emits an entry for every opcode in OP_CB_GETATTR..OP_CB_OFFLOAD,
but only opcodes with a nfsd4_callback_ops instance get incremented (via
nfsd4_run_cb() keyed on cb->cb_ops->opcode). Opcodes nfsd never sends
correctly report 0. The exception is op 11 / CB_SEQUENCE: it's written by
encode_cb_sequence4args() on every minorversion>0 callback, so it goes
out on the wire but is never counted. The dump always shows op 11 with
count 0, which could be misread as "none sent" rather than "not tracked."

Check what the forechannel counter does for SEQUENCE operations.


-- 
Chuck Lever

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 0/5] kdevops: fixes for new ansible and some nfsd default config changes

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 0/5] kdevops: fixes for new ansible and some nfsd default config changes

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 1/5] ansible: fix bare conditionals for ansible-core 2.20+ strict booleans
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes issues in Ansible playbooks where bare conditionals were used to evaluate variables that returned strings instead of booleans, causing fatal errors starting from Ansible-core version 2.20. The patch corrects the use of boolean filters and default values to ensure proper evaluation of these variables.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 2/5] ansible: replace deprecated top-level ansible_* facts with ansible_facts[]
series_items.patch_summary: This patch replaces deprecated top-level Ansible facts (e.g. ansible_os_family) with the new form using the ansible_facts dictionary, as required by Ansible-core 2.24. The change affects 109 playbook and template files across various roles in the tree.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 3/5] gitr: enable verbose logging in the test harness
series_items.patch_summary: This patch enables verbose logging in the test harness of the gitr suite, making it easier to diagnose issues when tests fail.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 4/5] nfsd: use more reasonable defaults for local VM testing
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the default settings for NFS server (nfsd) testing on local VMs to more reasonable values. The number of threads is increased from 8 to 128, and the lease time and grace period are reduced from 90 seconds to 30 seconds. Additionally, version 4.0 of the NFS protocol is enabled by default, and dynamic threading is used with newer kernels. Finally, the mountd service is configured to manage group IDs.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 5/5] devconfig: add some extra packages to the kdev tools
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds extra packages to the kdev tools installation process, specifically for Red Hat configurations. The added packages include strace, lsof, git, wireshark-cli, rsync, and time, which are useful tools for development and debugging purposes. This change only affects Red Hat distributions as the package names for other distros were unknown at the time of submission.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses issues introduced by new versions of Ansible and NFSd, including a backward-incompatible change in Ansible that requires fixes to conditionals. It also includes optional changes to existing workflows and configuration defaults for local VM testing. The patches remove redundant checks, replace deprecated facts with an array-based approach, enable verbose logging, and update package installations.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: kdevops-ng: graduating kdevops beyond Ansible
Re: kdevops-ng: graduating kdevops beyond Ansible
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch graduates kdevops-ng beyond its current reliance on Ansible, allowing for more flexible and efficient kernel testing. The existing approach using kconfig/makefiles is acknowledged as a 'square peg in a round hole'. The new direction aims to simplify the process by spawning a familiar Linux distribution, making it easier to troubleshoot issues.

review_comments.author: Daniel Gomez
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that Windmill's architecture is complex and not well-positioned in the market, but believes it has useful features for developer workflow orchestration and suggested exploring its potential to turn kdevops into a kernel regression workflow engine.

Reviewer Daniel Gomez expressed concerns about introducing too many new technologies at once (Nix and Windmill) and suggested a way forward: using Windmill as the replacement for Ansible, Makefiles, and Kconfig, while keeping the current toolkit for libvirt/libguestfs/Terraform paths.

Reviewer noted that userland test suites are also important for kernel testing and suggested using Nix to build and pin custom versions of tools, allowing for consistent builds between host and guest.
review_comments.raw_body:
100%.

AFAIK, even Windmill itself struggles a bit to position what it is in
the market. But I think they get some obviously useful things right for
developer workflow orchestration that nobody else does. BTW, before
landing on this project I also looked at others: cijoe [1], Kestra,
and, at a different level, task.dev and just.systems. And these are the
alternatives Windmill compares itself against [2], in case any of them
are familiar to you (none were to me).

For a quick sense of what Windmill is, here's their architecture
diagram: https://www.windmill.dev/docs/misc/architecture

The way I think of it: a GitLab runner plus the YAML pipeline in
.gitlab-ci.yml (or the GitHub Actions equivalent), but for your
development workflows, not your project's CI. Though it can obviously be
used for project's CI too. All in one package, with far more pipeline
("flow", in Windmill terms) features [3]. That last part is one that I'm
also interested in: we could turn kdevops into a kernel regression
workflow engine with git-bisect and generate reports we can submit
directly to the mailing list and maintainers (similar to 0-day). Yes, we
can already do this today with Luis's patches from last year (I tried
them and they work), but it's exactly the kind of workflow where you can
see Ansible isn't the right tool.

Link: https://cijoe.readthedocs.io/en/latest/# [1]
Link: https://www.windmill.dev/docs/compared_to/peers [2]
Link: https://www.windmill.dev/docs/core_concepts#flow-specific-features [3]

---

I understand, and I'm 100% with you: Nix (+ Windmill) is simply too
many new things at once.

The real benefit here is that the guest and the controller run the exact
same environment. You could compare it to virtme-ng with overlayfs,
where, AFAIK, you boot a guest off the host's rootfs (read-only, with
an overlay for writes), so the guest mirrors the host. The difference is
that with virtme-ng that shared environment is still whatever Debian or
Fedora you happen to be running, so it isn't pinned or reproducible;
with Nix it's the same shared environment (same /nix/store).

A way forward could be to use Windmill as the Ansible, Makefile, Kconfig
replacement or the Nix-based paths, and keep the current toolkit for the
libvirt/libguestfs/Terraform paths. Both can coexist.

---

But we also need changes on userland test suites so I think it matters
too. I use Nix for exactly that, to build and pin custom versions
of the test suites and tools (e.g. fstests, fio, bcc, etc.) that I can
run either baremetal or in a VM. The benefit is that the host and guest
share the same /nix/store (the guest mounts it over virtiofs), so a tool
you build on the host is the exact same build the guest runs.
